---
title: "From Chatting to Delegating"
subtitle: "Agentic AI for methodological research"
author: "Luka Šikić"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    width: 1200
    height: 720
    center: false
---

## Where you are now {.smaller}

You open a chat window. You craft a prompt. You get an answer.

Then **you** copy the code, run it, hit an error, paste the error back, and try again.

::: {.fragment}
You are the project manager. You hold the whole plan in your head.
The AI helps you with **one step at a time.**
:::

::: {.notes}
Set the baseline gently. Everyone here already does this. No judgment — name it so the contrast lands.
:::

## The leap {.smaller}

::: {.incremental}
- **Chat AI helps you _do_ the work.**
- **Agentic AI is _assigned_ the work.**
:::

::: {.fragment}
You stop crafting a prompt to get an answer.
You start **specifying a goal + constraints**, then **supervising** while it plans, acts, checks its
own work, and comes back when it's done.
:::

::: {.notes}
This single slide is the thesis. Don't rush it. The shift bundles three things at once — let later slides unpack them.
:::

## This is *your* kind of problem {.smaller}

Don't think "smarter chatbot." Think about what you already do every day:

::: {.incremental}
- You **brief and supervise an RA** — and you check their work.
- You write a **pre-analysis plan** before you touch the data.
- You **peer review** — the author doesn't get to grade their own paper.
- You keep an **audit trail** so the result is defensible and reproducible.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Agentic AI is built out of exactly these moves. You are *uniquely* equipped to lead it.
:::

## Four aches it goes after {.smaller}

::: {.incremental}
1. **It forgets** — between sessions, *why* you chose that notation or which bug you fixed.
2. **Quality wobbles** — one table is clean, the next is missing standard errors.
3. **Review is exhausting** — checking 140 slides by hand is soul-destroying.
4. **The deep one** — a spell-checker catches *teh*, but never a **flipped sign** in a theorem
   or a regression that is quietly wrong.
:::

::: {.notes}
Ache #4 is the emotional core for this audience. The mechanical errors are real and they've all been burned.
:::

## The whole thing in one breath {.smaller}

You describe what you want, in plain English. Claude behaves like a **general contractor**:

::: {.incremental}
- draws up a **blueprint** and gets your approval,
- **hires a crew** of specialists,
- **inspects** their work and sends them back to fix what's wrong,
- comes back to you only when the job **passes inspection**.
:::

::: {.fragment}
**You talk, Claude runs the site.** Everything else is plumbing built so you never think about plumbing.
:::

## The crew and the house {.smaller}

Five layers do all the work. Each has a plain-English nickname:

| Layer | Nickname | What it is |
|---|---|---|
| `CLAUDE.md` | **Constitution** | One short page of house rules, read every session |
| Rules | **Handbooks** | Manuals that appear only when relevant |
| Skills | **Buttons** | Named recipes (`/render`, `/proofread`, `/commit`) |
| Agents | **The crew** | Narrow specialists, each inspecting one thing |
| Settings + Hooks | **Automatic systems** | Permissions, and alarms that fire by themselves |

::: {.fragment}
Underneath it all: a **memory that survives** across sessions.
:::

## Idea 1 — specialists beat a generalist {.smaller}

Ask one assistant to watch grammar, layout, math, **and** code at once → it does a mediocre job at all four.

::: {.fragment}
Give every dimension its **own narrow inspector** → the errors that hide in plain sight finally get caught.
:::

::: {.fragment}
On a 140-slide deck: one agent catches the subscript that silently changed, one catches the
overlapping diagram label, one catches the script that ran *k = 10* while the slide says *k = 5*.
:::

## Idea 2 — the adversarial loop {.smaller}

The heart of the design.

::: {.incremental}
- A **critic** reads the work and lists every fault — but may **not touch the files.**
- A separate **fixer** makes the repairs — but may **not bless its own work.**
- The critic reads it again. Around they go, until a clean pass.
:::

::: {.fragment}
The critic can't fix, so it has no reason to go easy. The fixer can't sign off, so it can't declare
victory early. **This is just peer review** — and it kills AI's most common failure:
cheerfully announcing "looks good" when it isn't.
:::

## Two rules that keep it honest {.smaller}

**A score out of 100, read like a traffic light:**

::: {.incremental}
- **80** → safe to save your progress
- **90** → ready to ship
- **95** → excellence
- **below 80** → blocked until the faults are fixed
:::

::: {.fragment}
**A verification rule:** Claude may not call a task finished until it has actually **compiled,
rendered, or run** the thing. No announcing success from imagination.
:::

## What survives when the chat fills up {.smaller}

Long conversations get compressed — and the live chat is the thing that does **not** survive.
So anything worth keeping gets written to **disk**. Four labeled drawers:

::: {.incremental}
- **Constitution** — the standing rules.
- **Corrections log** — lessons, so the same mistake is never made twice.
- **Plans** — the strategy for the current task.
- **Session logs** — the *why* behind each choice. *Git records what; the log records why.*
:::

::: {.fragment}
The chat is a whiteboard that gets wiped. The drawers are a lab notebook that doesn't.
:::

## How a real job flows {.smaller}

Two moves, in order:

::: {.incremental}
1. **Plan before building.** Claude drafts a plan, saves it, and shows it to you *before* touching a
   file. You approve or revise.
2. **Contractor mode** takes the wheel: it works, verifies, runs the inspectors in parallel, applies
   fixes worst-first, scores the result, and loops until a clean pass.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Then it hands you a short summary and waits. **It commits nothing until you say so.**
:::

## It fails — and that's the point {.smaller}

A flawless demo is a sales pitch. Honest tools show their failure modes:

::: {.incremental}
- It can **hallucinate a citation** — so a verifier checks every reference against a real catalogue.
- It can be **confidently wrong** — so the critic re-audits and the gate **blocks** bad work.
- It can take a **wrong action** — so high-stakes steps wait for your approval.
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Autonomy is a dial, not a switch.** You decide where it's safe to let go.
:::

## A note on cost {.smaller}

Cheap models do the grunt work; the expensive model does the thinking.

::: {.incremental}
- **~70%** of small mechanical tasks → the fast, cheap model
- **~20%** of review work → the middle model
- **~10%** of real judgment → the top model
:::

::: {.fragment}
The bill drops **50–80%**, and the mechanical work loses nothing. You don't pay a senior professor
to reformat citations.
:::

## You don't need all of it {.smaller}

The full system is **18 agents, 52 skills, 32 rules.**

::: {.fragment}
That is the **ceiling, not the floor.**
:::

::: {.fragment}
Start with a **constitution** and **three buttons** — render, proofread, commit. Add a handbook or a
specialist only when you actually feel the lack of it.
:::

## These slides, by the way {.smaller}

This deck is a **plain-text file** in a folder.

::: {.incremental}
- An **agent helped write it.**
- It lives in **version control** — you can see exactly what changed, line by line.
- **One command rebuilds** the whole thing, code outputs and all.
:::

::: {.fragment}
The medium *is* the message: this is what "agent-authored, reproducible work" looks like.
:::

## If you forget everything else {.smaller}

::: {.incremental}
- **You talk, Claude runs the site.**
- **Specialists beat a generalist.**
- A **critic who can't fix**, paired with a **fixer who can't bless itself**, keeps the work honest.
- Anything you don't **write to disk** vanishes when the conversation fills up.
- **Start small** and let it grow.
:::

## Where to start this week {.smaller}

::: {.incremental}
- Pick **one tedious, mechanical task** you'd hand a first-year RA.
- Write it as a **task spec**: the goal, the constraints, what "done" looks like.
- Let the agent plan it; **approve the plan**; review the result against your own standards.
:::

::: {.fragment}
That one experiment teaches more than any slide. *(Resource kit: glossary + quality-gate card + cheat sheet.)*
:::
